episode_title: Mattia Peretti: Balancing AI Innovation with Journalistic Integrity

codes.description: The industry is moving past the initial generative AI hype toward a more pragmatic, strategy-driven phase focused on real value rather than experimentation for its own sake.
excerpts: I really feel it's like interesting this moment where We are beyond, as I was saying, the phase of let's try everything out. We're still trying things, but I see a healthy consolidation a little bit of the thinking into where is the real value.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Emphasis that AI adoption should prioritize value for readers and audiences, not just internal efficiency gains for the newsroom.
excerpts: And ideally more value for the users, not just for us.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: AI-generated article summaries serve as entry points that increase engagement rather than replacing full article consumption.
excerpts: it's an entry point kind of to get people interested. And you like, okay, this article is about this? Interesting. Let me read it. Let me find out more about it.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Even successful AI implementations like automated summaries require significant editorial work and human oversight.
excerpts: not you know, just asking ChatGPT or whatever to make the summary and then copy pasting it as it is with a lot of editorial work and human in the loop. still involved
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.code: European regional variation in AI adoption
codes.description: Different European regions show distinct patterns: UK mirrors US approach, Nordics are pragmatic experimenters, France and Germany have their own trajectories, Italy lags behind.
excerpts: I think Europe is interesting because you have the UK. as a market in itself that in some ways is similar to the US, I would say, as a Anglo-Saxon words. The Nordic countries were a lot of interesting experimentation come from, usually in very pragmatic and effective ways and other experiments coming up from Germany or France as well, not as much from my own country.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Core journalism-AI challenges identified in 2020 (summaries, archive accessibility) remain unsolved in 2024, suggesting these are evolving problems rather than ones with definitive solutions.
excerpts: it was striking to see how some of the challenges they were exploring back in twenty twenty. are still things we explore today. I remember the list was including how we might uh use AI to create AI powered summaries to create uh better engagement for users. That was already a thing at the time.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Traditional machine learning had already demonstrated significant value in newsrooms for paywall optimization, investigative data analysis, and personalization before generative AI emerged.
excerpts: if we look beyond the generative AI hype and the tools of the last uh, I don't know, what are we now? Sixteen, seventeen, eighteen months or something like that There was a lot of success already in the past few years with other machine learning tools for optimizing paywalls
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: AI adoption follows the same hype-driven pattern as previous technologies (VR, blockchain), where newsrooms rush to adopt without strategic reflection.
excerpts: Today is AI. Yesterday was virtual reality. The day before was blockchain. Of course AI has a bigger impact of the other technologies I just mentioned. That's that goes without saying. But I think the tendency is to just see a new thing and figure it out, okay, how we can use it.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Scaling up AI adoption should not be treated as a goal in itself; adoption should be ad hoc, driven by specific organizational goals, not technology-driven excitement.
excerpts: I want to think whether scaling up is should be a goal in itself. I think, you know, we tend that's a problem, I believe, in the way we have been approaching AI as an industry.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: News organizations should spend more time asking why they are adopting AI before rushing to how they will implement it.
excerpts: I think We could gain a lot as an industry, as practitioners, by spending a little bit more time on the why of things before rushing to how we make this happen, how we implement this technology.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Some newsrooms face technical and resource barriers so high that attempting AI implementation will be wasted effort, a reality that should be acknowledged honestly.
excerpts: the barrier to entry for the resources we have, for the technical capacity we have is so high. that it's gonna be wasted time trying to make it work for us.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Organizations tend to underestimate the limitations of large language models and overestimate the ease of AI solutions.
excerpts: we believe that a solution is easier than it is and actually limitations of large language models, for example. make it so we should not rely on them as much as we think.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: AI literacy, product experimentation, and principles/guidelines form an interdependent triangle where none can succeed without the other two.
excerpts: There is the AI literacy, there are the experiments in building products, and then everyone has been rushing to come up with guidelines and principles for generative AI and so on. Neither of these three things can exist and can have success without the other two, in my opinion.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Literacy, experimentation, and guidelines must develop simultaneously, not sequentially, because they inform each other.
excerpts: it's not something that we can take step by step. First, let me focus on this and only then I start with the other step the three things need to develop at the same time to inform each other as well.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: AI risks should be integrated into discussions from the start, not relegated to an afterthought at the end of a training session.
excerpts: I always think the risks should not be discussed as a you know as a separate thing. So I don't know, we organize a webinar, we have 50 minutes on all the amazing things. And then in the last five minutes we mentioned by the way there are risks as well. That's literally just not the way to go
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Not everyone needs to be an AI expert, but everyone needs basic literacy, combined with deeper expertise from a dedicated team or working group.
excerpts: It's not that everyone, every single person in a news organization needs to become an expert. in AI. I absolutely do not think that. We went through a few years ago in the journalism industry the time where everyone needed to become a coder and everyone needed to learn to code. I don't believe that's true.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: AI guidelines risk becoming empty performative exercises if not grounded in real literacy and experimentation.
excerpts: it's also a bit to avoid, you know, turning the writing of AI guidelines into a just a performative exercise, because everyone has them and we need to show readers that we are responsible about it.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Broad principles (the 'why') are more durable and effective than prescriptive guidelines (the 'how') because technology evolves too rapidly for specific rules.
excerpts: principles are more effective than guidelines per se for a number of reasons. A The technologies are evolving so fast that guidelines that are too prescriptive, they get old within a month.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Overly prescriptive guidelines risk signaling distrust of staff, communicating that management thinks they will misuse AI.
excerpts: I feel like the risk with putting to strict guidelines is to communicate in to your team that you don't trust them to know how to do the right thing. To really send the message then we as the management team or this AI working group, we are really afraid that you're gonna mess it up.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: The Guardian's AI principles were structured to address three audiences: internal teams, readers, and society/industry at large.
excerpts: the guardian principles for Generative AI talk about a message in a way for our team. Like these are our principles when it comes to internal implementations. These are our principles for towards our readers. We want to ensure them that we are going to do this in a responsible way
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Facilitating cross-organizational knowledge sharing is a foundational step for AI literacy, especially in large distributed organizations.
excerpts: even just bringing that knowledge sharing and facilitating that element, I think it's something that has been super interesting for me and something that all organizations should just spend time doing
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Within large organizations, teams working with AI operate in isolation from those unaware of AI use, creating dangerous knowledge gaps.
excerpts: There are groups of people working in AI and there are groups who have absolutely no clue that AI is even being used in the newsroom. And the biggest challenge is knowledge sharing.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: At Internews, Peretti worked to bridge teams excited about AI potential with teams concerned about AI harms, enriching both perspectives rather than choosing sides.
excerpts: not for a shouty match between the two, because one is more right than the other, but because both has so much to learn from one another and they need to understand. what the other point of view is.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: News organizations must not be naive about the profit-driven motives of tech companies in partnerships, since their mission fundamentally differs from journalism's mission.
excerpts: I think it's just very, very important that we're not naive about what are their goals. At the end of the day is these are for profit organizations. Their mission is profit. The mission is not the mission that we have as journalists.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Peretti expresses genuine difficulty taking a firm position on tech partnerships, understanding both sides while leaning toward tech companies being a net negative for society.
excerpts: I understand both sides and yet again I don't think you know the truth is on either side. I think we gotta be somewhere in between.
I think tech companies and what they're doing with AI development and so on. is a net negative if that exists for society as a whole.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Tech companies should pay for news data access because they are the richest organizations on the planet and can afford it; it is a matter of fairness.
excerpts: they should just pay to have access to the data that is allowing all of their fancy tools to function. Yes, as a matter of principle, but also because they can. These tech companies are the richest organizations and companies on the planet.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: News organizations have become hostage to tech platforms and must reclaim direct audience relationships to avoid dependence on platform priorities that shift unpredictably.
excerpts: We need to do much more work in building that relationship and being independent from any tool, from any platform. We have seen it, you know, with tech com the social media, you know, putting down in their priorities news and so not producing it anymore.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Peretti is transparent about his own funding relationships with Google and Microsoft, modeling the disclosure he advocates for the industry.
excerpts: all the work I've done at Journalism AI, so for the four years I was part of the team that was funded by the Google News Initiative... And Microsoft is funding my current work at the Craig Newmark. Graduate School of Journalism
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Personal worry that reliance on AI tools may erode cognitive capacities like memory, brainstorming, and independent information processing.
excerpts: I also worry if I rely too much on it, what muscle in my brain I'm not exercising anymore? And so what capacity do I lose to process information by myself?
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Peretti's limited personal AI use is framed not as technophobia but as deliberate application of the same 'why-first' principles he advocates for organizations.
excerpts: I've been preaching about spending more time on the Y zone. Well, I am spending my long time in the Y before I become a full-fledged adopter of myself in my daily life.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: As AI technology improves (e.g., GPT versions), organizations must plan for rapid iteration and be ready to swap underlying models as capabilities evolve.
excerpts: we may be build a tool right now that ex has GPT 3. 5, the next time something else comes out, we have to be able to adopt and be able to plan accordingly.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Despite ongoing challenges, five years of work have produced real progress in AI literacy across the industry, moving from near-impossible to promising.
excerpts: five years ago talking about AI literacy was I don't know talking about how we can get to the moon with our feet or something like that. While today luckily the attention on the topic, the initiatives that exist already. and you have been contributing uh to this as well in many ways are have already made a difference
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

codes.description: Even a self-described non-adopter turns to AI search (Perplexity) because traditional search (Google) has degraded in quality.
excerpts: search is terrible, so sometimes I do go to perplexity to help me find better information than Google right now offers.
codes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

episode_summary: This episode centers on Mattia Peretti's synthesized perspective from five years immersed in journalism-AI intersections, arguing that the industry has entered a post-hype pragmatic phase where strategic 'why' questions must precede technical 'how' implementation. His central framework is a triangle of interdependent literacy, experimentation, and principles that must develop in parallel -- with principles (not prescriptive guidelines) as the durable anchor because they survive technological change. The conversation reveals a deep ambivalence about tech company partnerships, with Peretti ultimately framing news organizations as having been taken 'hostage' by platform dependencies and arguing that reclaiming direct audience relationships is existentially important. Peretti's personal hesitancy to adopt AI in his own life mirrors his professional counsel of caution, illustrating how his 'why-first' philosophy operates at both organizational and individual scales.
surprises: Peretti, a prominent AI-in-journalism advocate, describes himself as not an early adopter and uses AI minimally in his personal life, embodying the caution he preaches professionally.

He frames tech companies' AI development as a 'net negative for society as a whole' despite having been funded by Google and Microsoft for years -- a striking admission given his funding relationships.

The observation that core AI-journalism challenges from 2020 remain unsolved in 2024 challenges the narrative that generative AI represents a revolutionary break rather than incremental progress on longstanding problems.
His argument that overly strict guidelines signal distrust of staff inverts the common framing where guidelines are seen as responsible governance.
tensions: Peretti advocates for engaging with AI while simultaneously worrying about cognitive atrophy from personal AI use -- a tension between professional necessity and personal values.
He acknowledges real opportunities from tech company partnerships while calling their overall impact on society 'net negative,' creating an unresolved position on how to practically engage.
The tension between needing to adopt AI to remain competitive versus his insistence that adoption should never be a goal in itself creates a difficult strategic needle to thread for newsroom leaders.
His funding disclosure (Google, Microsoft) alongside his increasingly critical stance toward tech companies reveals the structural dependency that permeates even critical voices in journalism.
Peretti champions principles over guidelines yet worked with The Guardian to publish a specific document -- suggesting that even principled approaches eventually require some codification.
